EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moved from the Central Rockies across the Central High Plains, producing an area of heavy snow over portions of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Snowfall up to 11 inches was reported.  Gusty north winds resulted in some blowing and drifting of snow.  Maximum snow totals in Nebraska included: Six inches in Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  In Kansas, six inches fell in Rawlins, Sherman and Wallace counties, seven inches in Thomas and Logan counties, eight inches in Sheridan county, ten inches in Decatur, Norton and Gove counties and eleven inches in Graham county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of snow was reported in Wallace county.

A powerful storm system brought heavy snow, ice and even blizzard conditions to a large portion of the central plains on December 7-9, 2009.  The greatest impact occurred across north central and northeast Kansas, southeast Nebraska, northwest and north central Missouri, and both southern and eastern Iowa, where strong winds gusting up to 45 mph produced blizzard conditions. The heaviest snowfall observed across extreme eastern Kansas was 8 inches in Atchison.||A review of the snow storm archives found this storm unusual in that it was the first snow storm that blizzard conditions were reported - wind speeds of 35 mph greater and wind-driven snow reducing visibilities to 1/4 mile or less sustained for three hours or more - to affect the forecast domain of the National Weather Service in Pleasant Hill, Missouri since the office opened in the 1992.  Though observed snowfall in the 10 to 14 inch range is uncommon, there have been several instances that snow amounts of these magnitudes have been observed in the past 15 years.  What is unusual is the combination of heavy snow and wind speeds exceeding 35 mph.  Since record keeping began, only five other instances have been recorded in which blizzard or near-blizzard conditions have impacted the forecast area for which the National Weather Service in Pleasant Hill has responsibility: February 5, 1924,  December 31, 1978-January 1, 1979,  December 1-2, 1985, January 18, 1996 and January 27, 1996.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of snow was observed across northern portions of the county.|Drifts of up to 4 feet were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of Northwest Kansas into Central and South Central Kansas during the early morning hours of May 8th, 2009.  This complex of thunmderstorms would intensify over South Central Kansas during the morning and become a prolific damaging wind producer.  This swath of damaging winds, (measured at 80 to 100 mph) cut a path of destruction from just north of Wichita, Kansas to Parsons, Kansas. This storm system would continue on into Southern Missouri and eventually Southern Illinois producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the El Dorado airport a small plane was flipped and a metal hanger collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two pronged winter storm moved across the region during the period of December 6th through December the 8th, 2009.  The initial system on December 6th spread a thin layer of freezing drizzle across portions of Central Kansas, which produced numerous automobile accidents with one leading to a death.  A more potent low pressure area tracked along the Kansas/Oklahoma border on December 7th and 8th, 2009.  This system led to a band of heavy snow across Central Kansas with a band of 6 to 12 inches along a line from Great Bend, Kansas to Salina, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to eight inches of snow fell across the county, with the heaviest amounts near Bushton, Kansas and Frederick, Kansas in the northern portion of the county.

A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of Northwest Kansas into Central and South Central Kansas during the early morning hours of May 8th, 2009.  This complex of thunmderstorms would intensify over South Central Kansas during the morning and become a prolific damaging wind producer.  This swath of damaging winds, (measured at 80 to 100 mph) cut a path of destruction from just north of Wichita, Kansas to Parsons, Kansas. This storm system would continue on into Southern Missouri and eventually Southern Illinois producing widespread damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of Northwest Kansas into Central and South Central Kansas during the early morning hours of May 8th, 2009.  This complex of thunmderstorms would intensify over South Central Kansas during the morning and become a prolific damaging wind producer.  This swath of damaging winds, (measured at 80 to 100 mph) cut a path of destruction from just north of Wichita, Kansas to Parsons, Kansas. This storm system would continue on into Southern Missouri and eventually Southern Illinois producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty National Weather Service employee reported estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two pronged winter storm moved across the region during the period of December 6th through December the 8th, 2009.  The initial system on December 6th spread a thin layer of freezing drizzle across portions of Central Kansas, which produced numerous automobile accidents with one leading to a death.  A more potent low pressure area tracked along the Kansas/Oklahoma border on December 7th and 8th, 2009.  This system led to a band of heavy snow across Central Kansas with a band of 6 to 12 inches along a line from Great Bend, Kansas to Salina, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle led to numerous accidents on elevated roadways in and around Hutchinson, Kansas.  One accident occurred on US highway 56 east of Elinwood when two vehicles collided.  One driver had to be extricated from her car.  Both drivers were injured and taken to local hospitals. Another accident involved two vehicles, with 2 passengers sustaining numerous injuries and taken a local hospitals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the area.  A tight pressure gradient behind the front allowed for strong winds to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was observed in Wakeeney.

Thunderstorms first developed during the late afternoon hours on Sunday, June 7th over Northern Oklahoma where a dryline and cold front intersected. As the storms tracked northeast, they encountered richer moisture and higher instability which allowed them to increase in intensity.  The first round of storms Sunday evening June 7th, produced a swatch of severe winds.  Around midnight, June 8th, a large storm developed over Reno County that produced significant wind damage in South Hutchinson along with golf ball size hail.  Damage surveys concluded the damage was a miroburst with winds between 100-120 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts along Highway 42.

Thunderstorms first developed during the late afternoon hours on Sunday, June 7th over Northern Oklahoma where a dryline and cold front intersected. As the storms tracked northeast, they encountered richer moisture and higher instability which allowed them to increase in intensity.  The first round of storms Sunday evening June 7th, produced a swatch of severe winds.  Around midnight, June 8th, a large storm developed over Reno County that produced significant wind damage in South Hutchinson along with golf ball size hail.  Damage surveys concluded the damage was a miroburst with winds between 100-120 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two maple trees were knocked down.  One tree was five inches in diameter and the other was four inches in diameter, near the Riverside subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old male was struck by lightning when he ventured outdoors where some tents were pitched.  He made a full recovery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old male was struck and severely injured by a lightning bolt.  He was going outside where some tents were pitch.  CPR had to be performed as he had not pulse or respiration when rescuers reached him.  Miraculously he made a full recovery after about 2 weeks in the hospital.

A long-track supercell moved southwest from Nebraska into western Kansas, eastern Colorado and ultimately New Mexico.  Large damaging hail fell in eastern Colorado, breaking windows in homes and piling hail into two foot drifts on the north side of homes.  When the storm crossed Interstate 70 just east of Burlington, Colorado, numerous cars received broken windows and windshields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size and time estimated.  All windows on east side of home broken.  Damage also occurred to a pickup truck and an SUV.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size and time estimated.  Thirty five chickens were killed by hail, and two horses injured. A vehicle had its windshield broken by strong wind and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms first developed during the late afternoon hours on Sunday, June 7th over Northern Oklahoma where a dryline and cold front intersected. As the storms tracked northeast, they encountered richer moisture and higher instability which allowed them to increase in intensity.  The first round of storms Sunday evening June 7th, produced a swatch of severe winds.  Around midnight, June 8th, a large storm developed over Reno County that produced significant wind damage in South Hutchinson along with golf ball size hail.  Damage surveys concluded the damage was a miroburst with winds between 100-120 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts around 2.50 inches caused county roads to be barricaded to the north of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved out of eastern Colorado late in the evening and several of these storms were significant supercell storms.  One supercell in particular started near Pueblo Colorado during the late afternoon on the 11th and ended up south of Dodge City by sunrise on the 12th.  Large hail and damaging winds destroyed crops and caused significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst was evident on radar and by ground survey south of Dodge City.  One pivot irrigation sprinkler was rolled over and a grain bin was destroyed and carried about 3/4 of a mile.  A 1/2 mile stretch of power poles were blown down also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved out of eastern Colorado late in the evening and several of these storms were significant supercell storms.  One supercell in particular started near Pueblo Colorado during the late afternoon on the 11th and ended up south of Dodge City by sunrise on the 12th.  Large hail and damaging winds destroyed crops and caused significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second downburst was recorded by radar and verified by ground survey south of Dodge City and south of another damage area.  In this area, three pivot irrigation sprinklers were overturned, several power poles were broken and a 1/4 mile stretch of cedar trees were uprooted.  The damage area was about 3/4 mile wide and 2 1/2 miles long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved out of eastern Colorado late in the evening and several of these storms were significant supercell storms.  One supercell in particular started near Pueblo Colorado during the late afternoon on the 11th and ended up south of Dodge City by sunrise on the 12th.  Large hail and damaging winds destroyed crops and caused significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a severe thunderstorm produced widespread damage in town.  Many large trees were uprooted and there was also some roof damage.  Hail also occurred and there were many birds and small animals laying dead after the storm.  In addition, about 2.50 of rain fell in 30 minutes causing flooding of city streets. Severe thunderstorms moved out of eastern Colorado late in the evening and several of these storms were significant supercell storms.  One supercell in particular started near Pueblo Colorado during the late afternoon on the 11th and ended up south of Dodge City by sunrise on the 12th.  Large hail and damaging winds destroyed crops and caused significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.50 fell in about 30 minutes causing flooding of city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved out of eastern Colorado late in the evening and several of these storms were significant supercell storms.  One supercell in particular started near Pueblo Colorado during the late afternoon on the 11th and ended up south of Dodge City by sunrise on the 12th.  Large hail and damaging winds destroyed crops and caused significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accompanied by 70 to 90 mph winds and did extensive damage to vehicles, buildings, crops and wildlife.  The largest stones were a bit bigger than a baseball with the majority golfball sized.  At one location 2 miles southeast of Cimarron the siding was stripped off a house.  Many fields of alfalfa and wheat were completely mowed down by the wind and hail.  Numerous rabbits and birds were laying dead where cover had once stood.  Also, there were 5 pivot irrigation sprinklers overturned and several trees uprooted.  Trees that were not uprooted were completely stripped of leaves and even pine trees were stripped of their needles in some cases.

A slow moving storm system produced 6 to 12 inches of heavy wet snow across parts of Northeast Kansas.  The heaviest snow and greatest impact occurred along and north of a Concordia to Hiawatha line where numerous power outages due to downed power lines were reported.  Very little in the way of snowfall accumulation occurred south of this line since air temperatures were in the lower to middle 30s and the significant accumulation occurred in areas of heavy precipitation while other areas that received snow saw it melt as it fell.  Damage estimates of 1.6 million dollars included damage to power poles across Washington, Marshall, Republic and Cloud counties with the worst damage across Washington and Marshall Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of heavy wet snow estimated on the ground in Morrill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the morning hours on Christmas Day as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains.  The system deepened across the Southern Plains and along the Missouri River, before shifting north and settling in across Iowa on Christmas Day.  Being oriented on the backside of the upper low pressure system allowed portions of north central Kansas to catch the brunt of the combination of snowfall and very strong winds associated with the storm.   While only a few inches of snow fell across the area, the combination of the falling snow and the blowing and drifting caused by the accompanying northwest winds of 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph produced blizzard white-out conditions.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.

Energy associated with a strong system moving out of the Desert Southwest brought a mixed bag of wintry precipitation to the area.  Through the morning hours, freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and snow were observed across north central Kansas.  Ice accumulations due to freezing rain ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, and confined to an area east of a line from Smith Center to Osborne.  By late morning much of the precipitation had transitioned to snow, which then continued well into the afternoon and evening hours.  Most of the area saw little to no snow accumulation, but northwest portions of the area in Phillips, Smith, and Rooks Counties did receive anywhere from 1 to 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm system brought heavy snow, significant sleet and light freezing rain accumulations in addition to strong winds to southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri Christmas Eve into early Christmas Day.  Snowfall accumulations of four to six inches were common on top of sleet accumulations of one quarter to one inch. Near blizzard conditions developed across southeast Kansas and far southwest and west central Missouri as the surface low tracked nearly due north from Arkansas to northeast Missouri.  Sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with higher gusts were common reducing the visibility, and causing blowing and drifting snow.  Wind chill values were well below zero. This winter storm had a severe impact on holiday travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of four to six inches in addition to sleet accumulations of one half to one inch and a minor accumulation of freezing rain. Winds of 20 to 35 mph resulted in near blizzard conditions at times and dangerous wind chills. Holiday travel was severely impacted.

While only a few inches of snow fell across the area, the combination of the falling snow and the blowing and drifting caused by the accompanying northwest winds of 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph produced blizzard white-out conditions.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.

A slow moving storm system produced 6 to 12 inches of heavy wet snow across parts of Northeast Kansas.  The heaviest snow and greatest impact occurred along and north of a Concordia to Hiawatha line where numerous power outages due to downed power lines were reported.  Very little in the way of snowfall accumulation occurred south of this line since air temperatures were in the lower to middle 30s and the significant accumulation occurred in areas of heavy precipitation while other areas that received snow saw it melt as it fell.  Damage estimates of 1.6 million dollars included damage to power poles across Washington, Marshall, Republic and Cloud counties with the worst damage across Washington and Marshall Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow measured at 12 inches four miles southeast of Morrowville in Washington County.  Residents are without power and one mile of power poles are reported to be snapped from the weight of snow on lines.  Damage estimate of 1.6 million is for combined county total including Washington, Marshall, Republic and Cloud counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flooding occurred across southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks. The heavy rainfall was associated with several rounds of efficient rain producing stratiform events along a stalled warm front. Isolated convective elements embedded in the system and a few ahead of the system also produce some enhanced rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low water crossing near the intersection of County Road 700 and 30th Street was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm system brought heavy snow, significant sleet and light freezing rain accumulations in addition to strong winds to southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri Christmas Eve into early Christmas Day.  Snowfall accumulations of four to six inches were common on top of sleet accumulations of one quarter to one inch. Near blizzard conditions developed across southeast Kansas and far southwest and west central Missouri as the surface low tracked nearly due north from Arkansas to northeast Missouri.  Sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with higher gusts were common reducing the visibility, and causing blowing and drifting snow.  Wind chill values were well below zero. This winter storm had a severe impact on holiday travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of four to six inches in addition to sleet accumulations of one half to one inch and a minor accumulation of freezing rain. Winds of 20 to 35 mph resulted in near blizzard conditions at times and dangerous wind chills. Holiday travel was severely impacted.

A strong storm system swept through the Central Plains during the Christmas holiday, bringing moderate to heavy snow and strong winds. The event started out with light freezing rain, but cooler air quickly worked its way into the mid levels of the atmosphere and the precipitation changed to snow. Areas of east central Kansas received up to a quarter inch of ice before the moderate to heavy snow began falling on Christmas Eve. Just after midnight on the 25th strong winds pushed through the area bringing blizzard conditions to far eastern Kansas. Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell between December 24th and December 25th across Marshall county.  Spotters in Blue Rapids reported 9.5 to 10 inches of snow. Wind gusts in and around that area were as high as 50 mph, and trained spotters throughout Marshall county reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours in the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Aside from the lowered visibility, blowing and drifting snow caused treacherous travel conditions. Many roads were impassable as a result of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm battered much of Northeast Kansas dumping up to 15 inches of snow across parts of the area.  Many areas received over 6 inches of snow during the storm which began on Monday night and persisted into the early morning hours of Wednesday. Areas along and southeast of an Abilene to Manhattan to Hiawatha line experienced periods of freezing rain and sleet during the day Tuesday, December 8th, which limited the snowfall accumulations however northwest of this line the precipitation primarily fell as snow and it was heavy during the day Tuesday.  Although the heavy snows ended by Tuesday night, extremely strong northwest winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph developed and produced near blizzard conditions across parts of the area during the early morning hours on Wednesday.  Wind chill values dropped into the -10 to -20 degree range early on Wednesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports throughout Ottawa county indicated that between 8 and 12 inches of snow fell between December 7th and December 9th. The highest snow total from Ottawa county came out of Minneapolis, KS where nearly 12 inches of snow was reported.  Gusty winds, along with the snow, created near blizzard conditions throughout the county. The snow and winds also resulted in many cars sliding off roads and into ditches.

The highest report, 13 inches, received at the National Weather Service office in Topeka, KS came from just west of Oketo, KS. The Marysville city water plant recorded 12.4 inches of snow over the three day event. According to local newspapers, the county emergency manager reported 14 to 15 inch amounts from across the county. Strong winds caused drifts which reached 6-8 feet in height and created near blizzard conditions which made travel through the county difficult. Four semi-trailer rigs were blown over in separate incidents. One of the rigs was carrying a load of pigs. The cargo came loose when the rig toppled over, and took several hours to round up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm battered much of Northeast Kansas dumping up to 15 inches of snow across parts of the area.  Many areas received over 6 inches of snow during the storm which began on Monday night and persisted into the early morning hours of Wednesday. Areas along and southeast of an Abilene to Manhattan to Hiawatha line experienced periods of freezing rain and sleet during the day Tuesday, December 8th, which limited the snowfall accumulations however northwest of this line the precipitation primarily fell as snow and it was heavy during the day Tuesday.  Although the heavy snows ended by Tuesday night, extremely strong northwest winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph developed and produced near blizzard conditions across parts of the area during the early morning hours on Wednesday.  Wind chill values dropped into the -10 to -20 degree range early on Wednesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports out of Scandia, Belleville, and Norway indicated over 12 inches of snow fell throughout the county between the dates of December 7th and December 9th. The snow finally ended during the early morning hours on December 9th.  Many cars slid off of roads and into ditches due to the slick conditions and high winds. One vehicle accident was caused when a passenger car struck the rear of a semi which slowed due to the lowered visibility.

Local roads were nearly impassable due to the heavy snow and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system swept through the Central Plains during the Christmas holiday, bringing moderate to heavy snow and strong winds. The event started out with light freezing rain, but cooler air quickly worked its way into the mid levels of the atmosphere and the precipitation changed to snow. Areas of east central Kansas received up to a quarter inch of ice before the moderate to heavy snow began falling on Christmas Eve. Just after midnight on the 25th strong winds pushed through the area bringing blizzard conditions to far eastern Kansas. Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell between December 24th and December 25th across Nemaha county.   A spotter near Corning reported 16.0 inches of snow; while spotters near Seneca and Baileyville reported 13.5 to 14.0 inches of snow. Wind gusts in and around that area were as high as 50 mph, and trained spotters throughout Nemaha county reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours in the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Aside from the lowered visibility, blowing and drifting snow caused treacherous travel conditions. Many roads were impassable as a result of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm developed over the central High Plains early on the 23rd, producing an area of moderate to heavy snow generally southeast of a line from Russell Springs in Logan County to Norcatur in Decatur County.  County snow totals included 7-8 inches in Logan County, 6-8 inches in Gove County, 5-7 inches in Sheridan County, six inches in Graham County, 5-7 inches in Decatur county and near 8 inches in Norton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals included seven inches in Dresden and five inches in Oberlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought a combination freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and snow to extreme southeast Kansas January 26 and 27, 2009.  Freezing drizzle and light freezing rain developed at the onset of the event. While freezing rain accretion was light it did   result in multiple traffic accidents. The wintry precipitation transitioned to sleet then snow during the event.  Sleet accumulations up to 2 inches were followed by 2 to 4 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant accumulation of a wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow resulted in treacherous travel conditions.  Ice accretion of near one quarter inch or less was followed by 2 to 4 inches of sleet and snow.

Just after midnight on the 25th strong winds pushed through the area bringing blizzard conditions to far eastern Kansas. Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling during the daytime hours on the 24th, with around .25 inch of ice accumulating on surfaces. By the evening of the 24th moderate to heavy snow fell across Anderson county. By the time the snow ended, places near Garnett reported around 5 inches of snow. Wind gusts in and around that area were as high as 50 mph and trained spotters throughout Anderson county reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours in the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Aside from the lowered visibility, blowing and drifting snow caused treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm battered much of Northeast Kansas dumping up to 15 inches of snow across parts of the area.  Many areas received over 6 inches of snow during the storm which began on Monday night and persisted into the early morning hours of Wednesday. Areas along and southeast of an Abilene to Manhattan to Hiawatha line experienced periods of freezing rain and sleet during the day Tuesday, December 8th, which limited the snowfall accumulations however northwest of this line the precipitation primarily fell as snow and it was heavy during the day Tuesday.  Although the heavy snows ended by Tuesday night, extremely strong northwest winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph developed and produced near blizzard conditions across parts of the area during the early morning hours on Wednesday.  Wind chill values dropped into the -10 to -20 degree range early on Wednesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nemaha county received a foot of snow between the dates of December 7th and December 9th. The highest report of snow received by the National Weather Service office in Topeka was 12 inches out of Corning, KS. Heavy snow in conjunction with strong, gusty winds made for near blizzard conditions. Travel was treacherous throughout the region, as the snow and wind resulted in numerous vehicle slide-offs. No injuries were reported from these slide-offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong bow echo feature brought 70-80 mph winds into Manhattan Kansas, early on the morning of July 12.  No reports of severe weather were received prior to the storm's entrance into Manhattan, but when the storm entered the city it caused tree and structure damage across a wide swath through the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was observed on the extreme northwestern edge of town.  The damage path went right through the center of town and created the worst damage to the center part of town just south and southeast of town.  A roof was blown off of a motel and caused damage to cars in the parking lot. Numerous trees, up to 48 inches in diameter were snapped at the base, and some fell on cars and houses.  The visible damage path ended on the southeast part of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was nickel to quarter sized.

An upper low across Eastern Nebraska and cyclonic curvature across Central Kansas lead to the development of a cluster of southward moving supercells on the evening of September 3rd, 2009.  The supercell thunderstorms lead to a swath of large hail, up to baseball size, and damaging winds across portions of Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of large hail moved south across the western half of the county, causing extensive damage to crops, homes and vehicles. Windows were busted out of the homes, and leaves were shreaded off of the trees.  The hail was so plentiful that Highway 56 became covered white with hail.  This caused a propane truck to slide off the road into a ditch just east of the Barton/Rice county line, destoying the truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced 6 to 12 inches of heavy wet snow across parts of Northeast Kansas.  The heaviest snow and greatest impact occurred along and north of a Concordia to Hiawatha line where numerous power outages due to downed power lines were reported.  Very little in the way of snowfall accumulation occurred south of this line since air temperatures were in the lower to middle 30s and the significant accumulation occurred in areas of heavy precipitation while other areas that received snow saw it melt as it fell.  Damage estimates of 1.6 million dollars included damage to power poles across Washington, Marshall, Republic and Cloud counties with the worst damage across Washington and Marshall Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 7 inches of heavy wet snow reported 6 miles east of Marysville.  Rolling Hills Electric Cooperative  stated at around 750 power poles were snapped due to downed lines in Republic, Washington and Marshall Counties. The 1.6 million dollar damage estimate is for all counties combined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp upper level trough shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and across the Central Plains, bringing a round of heavy snow to north central Kansas.  Lift present ahead of the main system started to bring snow to the area during the early morning hours, dropping a few inches of snow.  After a brief lull in the activity, snow increased once again during the afternoon as the main system moved into the area.  Snow continued to fall into the evening and overnight hours, and by the time it had come to an end the next morning, 6 to 12 inches had fallen across the area.  Along with the snowfall, strong northwest winds behind a passing surface cold front with sustained speeds near 30 mph and gusts near 45 mph caused blowing and drifting of the snow, as well as near white out conditions.  Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

Energy associated with a strong system moving out of the Desert Southwest brought a mixed bag of wintry precipitation to the area.  Through the morning hours, freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and snow were observed across north central Kansas.  Ice accumulations due to freezing rain ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, and confined to an area east of a line from Smith Center to Osborne.  By late morning much of the precipitation had transitioned to snow, which then continued well into the afternoon and evening hours.  Most of the area saw little to no snow accumulation, but northwest portions of the area in Phillips, Smith, and Rooks Counties did receive anywhere from 1 to 3 inches of snow.

Areas along and southeast of an Abilene to Manhattan to Hiawatha line experienced periods of freezing rain and sleet during the day Tuesday, December 8th, which limited the snowfall accumulations however northwest of this line the precipitation primarily fell as snow and it was heavy during the day Tuesday.  Although the heavy snows ended by Tuesday night, extremely strong northwest winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph developed and produced near blizzard conditions across parts of the area during the early morning hours on Wednesday.  Wind chill values dropped into the -10 to -20 degree range early on Wednesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snow report, 10.5 inches, out of Pottawatomie county came just northwest of Onaga, KS. Heavy snow blanketed the area over a three day stretch beginning on December 7th and endinig on December 9th. Strong winds accompanied the storm, which brought near blizzard conditions for periods during the event. The strong storm made travel treacherous and aided in many vehicles sliding off the road into nearby ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong storm system wreaked havoc on a large portion of the central United States during one of the heaviest travel periods of the year. Well before the arrival of the storm, warm air surging north from the Gulf of Mexico brought torrential rains and even thunderstorms to central and southern Missouri on Wednesday (Dec 23rd). Rainfall across central Missouri averaged between 1.5 and 2 inches, with Whiteman Air Force base reporting just over 2 inches. This rain generated an unusually late bout of river flooding across that region as most of the ground was still frozen, resulting in excessive runoff. ||Shortly after midnight Thursday (Dec 24th), an arctic cold front swept through the area, sending temperatures on a downward spiral through the 40s, 30s, 20s and eventually landing in the teens by nightfall on Christmas Eve. With warm air still present aloft, the first round of precipitation fell mainly in the form of sleet, with some freezing rain across central Missouri. Sleet (also known as ice pellets) forms when snow melts while falling through a layer of warm air several thousand feet above the ground. The cold rain then refreezes into ice pellets as it encounters a second layer of very cold air near the ground. The sleet came down particularly heavy at times from eastern portions of the Kansas City metro, south and toward Sedalia and Clinton, with ice pellet accumulations upwards of 2 inches in some areas.||By mid evening, enough cold air had wrapped in aloft to change all the precipitation over to snow. Heavy snow blanketed much of western Missouri and eastern Kansas, with winds of 30 to 45 mph resulting in blizzard conditions from Kansas City to the Iowa and Nebraska borders -- west into the Great Plains. Snowfall amounts averaged between 6 and 8 inches along and west of Interstate 35, with drifts 3 to 8 feet reported in many areas. Travel was crippled across many areas with portions of Interstate 29 closed north of St. Joseph. A white Christmas for many, came at the expense of the first blizzard to impact the Kansas City area since 1989, and the first blizzard warning issued for Kansas City since 1982!||Overall for the 4 day period, a broad area of the lower Missouri River Valley region experienced snowfall totals in excess of 8 inches.  Hardest hit areas of northwest Missouri and northeast Kansas received as much as 11 to 14 inches of snow during this storm.  Generally, central and north central Missouri experienced lower snowfall totals, with most areas receiving anywhere from 3 to 5 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to eight inches of snow was observed across the county. Strong northwest winds caused blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A double-barreled winter storms affected the Kansas region from the predawn hours on the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 25th. The first piece of energy early on the 23rd brought widespread freezing rain to portions of mainly central Kansas, with ice accumulations reaching one-quarter inch in spots. There was a relative lull in the action for much of the 23rd through early on the 24th, before the second and more powerful piece of energy spread widespread sleet and heavy snow for locations mainly east of Interstate 135. Precipitation started as sleet and freezing rain mixed with snow during the mid morning hours on the 24th. The sleet was heavy at times, accumulating up to 2 inches in spots. The wintry mix gradually changed over to all snow from west to east during the afternoon hours, and persisted into the early morning hours on the 25th. The snow was heavy at times, with accumulations ranging from 4 to 8 inches generally along and east of the flint hills. The heavy snow in concert with strong and gusty north to northwest winds resulted in near blizzard conditions at times. Needless to say, travel conditions were treacherous, with numerous accidents and slide-offs occurring across the area, producing a handful of injuries and two known fatalities. The following counties were included in a disaster emergency declaration: Allen, Chautauqua, Elk, Greenwood, Labette, Montgomery, Neosho, Wilson and Woodson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the mid to late morning hours on the 24th changed to all snow during the afternoon hours, and continued into the early morning hours on the 25th. The sleet and snow was heavy at times. Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 7 inches, highest across northeast portions of the county. The heavy snow in concert with strong and gusty northerly winds resulted in near blizzard conditions at times. A few minor vehicle accidents occurred across the county.

Two weather-related accidents were reported, one a couple miles south of Iola, and another south of Gas. It is unknown whether or not these accidents produced injuries. The cost to clear snow and ice across the county was about $38,000. The Iola Register contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching from the west in concert with relatively warm and moist air overrunning a cold arctic airmass entrenched over the area resulted in periodic bouts of freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and light snow across portions of south-central and southeast Kansas from around noon on the 26th to the late evening hours on the 27th. Southeast Kansas was hardest hit, where up to one-half inch of ice and sleet accumulated. Areas further west received mainly a light glaze of ice. Additionally, portions of south-central and southeast Kansas received anywhere from a dusting to two inches of snow accumulation. Needless to say, very slick road conditions were common, which aided in numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to about one-quarter inch across mainly southern portions of the county, due to periodic freezing drizzle and freezing rain. Light snow accumulated to about one-half inch. Numerous automobile accidents occurred countywide, but no reports of injuries were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice due to freezing drizzle, sleet and light snow resulted in an injury accident on Highway 160 just west of Bluff Road. The passenger, a 46-year-old Wichita man, was also injured. Information courtesy of the Hutchinson News. Numerous other accidents likely occurred across the county due to the widespread slick conditions, but specific details are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of Northwest Kansas into Central and South Central Kansas during the early morning hours of May 8th, 2009.  This complex of thunmderstorms would intensify over South Central Kansas during the morning and become a prolific damaging wind producer.  This swath of damaging winds, (measured at 80 to 100 mph) cut a path of destruction from just north of Wichita, Kansas to Parsons, Kansas. This storm system would continue on into Southern Missouri and eventually Southern Illinois producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home was blown 40 feet and ended up wrapped around a large tree and was totally demolished.  A 54 year old woman of the home was found in the wreckage and died.  A church in town was totally destroyed and also the town post office was a total loss after it collapsed. Numerous trees were also down across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system swept through the Central Plains during the Christmas holiday, bringing moderate to heavy snow and strong winds. The event started out with light freezing rain, but cooler air quickly worked its way into the mid levels of the atmosphere and the precipitation changed to snow. Areas of east central Kansas received up to a quarter inch of ice before the moderate to heavy snow began falling on Christmas Eve. Just after midnight on the 25th strong winds pushed through the area bringing blizzard conditions to far eastern Kansas. Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling during the daytime hours on the 24th, with around .25 inch of ice accumulating on surfaces. By the evening of the 24th moderate to heavy snow fell between December 24th and December 25th across Osage county. Trained spotters in Carbondale reported around 10 inches of snow; while spotters near Osage City reported 13 inches. Wind gusts in and around that area were as high as 50 mph and trained spotters throughout Osage county reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours in the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Aside from the lowered visibility, blowing and drifting snow caused treacherous travel conditions. Many roads were impassable as a result of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough moved east through the western Plains on Tuesday, December 8th, 2009.  Snow formed early in the morning and picked up in intensity by mid-morning.  By Tuesday evening, 6 to 9 inches of snow had fallen north of a line from Scott City to Dighton to Jetmore to Larned.  The highest snow amount was 9 inches and this was reported at a location 13 miles west-northwest of Hays in Ellis county.  From 2 to 4 inches fell in a band about 50 miles wide stretching from Garden City to Pratt.  Lessor amounts fell south of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at a location 10 miles WNW of Jetmore.

Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two pronged winter storm moved across the region during the period of December 6th through December the 8th, 2009.  The initial system on December 6th spread a thin layer of freezing drizzle across portions of Central Kansas, which produced numerous automobile accidents with one leading to a death.  A more potent low pressure area tracked along the Kansas/Oklahoma border on December 7th and 8th, 2009.  This system led to a band of heavy snow across Central Kansas with a band of 6 to 12 inches along a line from Great Bend, Kansas to Salina, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle led to a few auto accidents across the county.  A pickup slid off of Kansas Highway 4 east of Hoisington, Kansas.  The driver and passenger were treated and released. Three other minor accidents occurred in and around Great Bend, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough moved east through the western Plains on Tuesday, December 8th, 2009.  Snow formed early in the morning and picked up in intensity by mid-morning.  By Tuesday evening, 6 to 9 inches of snow had fallen north of a line from Scott City to Dighton to Jetmore to Larned.  The highest snow amount was 9 inches and this was reported at a location 13 miles west-northwest of Hays in Ellis county.  From 2 to 4 inches fell in a band about 50 miles wide stretching from Garden City to Pratt.  Lessor amounts fell south of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow fell at a location in Liebenthal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system moved out of the Southwest United States into the the southern plains during the late afternoon into the early evening hours of February 10th, 2009.  Warm moist air ahead of this system combined with strong dynamics across portions of South Central and Southeast Kansas to produce low topped supercells that produced large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported nickel sized hail.

By Tuesday evening, 6 to 9 inches of snow had fallen north of a line from Scott City to Dighton to Jetmore to Larned.  The highest snow amount was 9 inches and this was reported at a location 13 miles west-northwest of Hays in Ellis county.  From 2 to 4 inches fell in a band about 50 miles wide stretching from Garden City to Pratt.  Lessor amounts fell south of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at a location 13 miles north of Ness City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system swept through the Central Plains during the Christmas holiday, bringing moderate to heavy snow and strong winds. The event started out with light freezing rain, but cooler air quickly worked its way into the mid levels of the atmosphere and the precipitation changed to snow. Areas of east central Kansas received up to a quarter inch of ice before the moderate to heavy snow began falling on Christmas Eve. Just after midnight on the 25th strong winds pushed through the area bringing blizzard conditions to far eastern Kansas. Wind gusts as high as 48 mph were observed at Topeka's Forbes Field, and wind gusts as high as 54 mph were observed in Emporia, KS.  The winds not only lowered visibilities to below 1/4 mile for several hours, but blew the soft powdery snow across the roadways making travel treacherous, and nearly impossible. When the snow finally stopped up to 10 inches fell across the northeastern portion of Kansas. Getting accurate snow measurements was difficult due to the blowing and drifting nature of the snow, so totals may have been higher in some locations.  As a result of the blowing and drifting snow, drifts around 5 to 6 feet deep formed against fences, houses, barns and road embankments. The snow gradually tapered off through the 26th. The storm system stalled over Iowa and even retrograded a bit to the west bringing another 2 days of light snow with little accumulation. By the 27th most of the area saw the snow diminish completely. The snow from Dec 23rd through December 26th allowed both Topeka and Concordia to set records for most snowfall in the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling during the daytime hours on the 24th, with around .25 inch of ice accumulating on surfaces. By the evening of the 24th moderate to heavy snow fell across Douglas county. By the time the snow ended, Lawrence received around 9 inches of snow, and Lecompton reported around 10 inches of snow. The Lawrence ASOS reported a wind gust of 45 mp at 1:03 am on December 25th. Trained spotters throughout Douglas county reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours in the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Aside from the lowered visibility, blowing and drifting snow caused treacherous travel conditions. Several vehicle accidents were reported in the county due to the treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level storm system, dropped southeast through the heart of the country, on the last day of February. As this storm dived southward during the day, it was able to tap additional moisture from the deep south. This, combined with very cold temperatures aloft, allowed for unusually high snow amounts compared to their melted counterparts. A narrow axis of moderate snow pivoted along the Kansas Missouri border for nearly 8 hours, resulting in some of the highest snow totals of the season. A swath of 4 to 6 inches of snow, was observed in Leavenworth and Doniphan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported in Troy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving upper level low pressure system shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and onto the plains during the daytime hours of March 23rd. A well vertically stacked system, it brought strong winds as well as thunderstorms to portions of North Central Kansas. With strong forcing and a tight pressure gradient across the area, this lead to widespread sustained wind speeds of 35 to 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph, which lingered into the evening hours. These winds blew some dust around, which caused some problems for travelers. A few strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, dropping pea to penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accompanied by winds estimated at 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed ahead of an eastward moving dryline and cold front that pushed through Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Discrete thunderstorms merged into a convective line as they moved through the Topeka County Warning Area.  Hail was the primary severe report received locally, with the largest stones reported one and a half inches in diameter.  Sporadic thunderstorm wind gusts greater than 50kts were also received, and one tornado was reported. A swath of straight line wind damage was noted to have occurred in close proximity to the tornado path. An official NWS Storm Survey corroborated by Topeka WSR-88d indicated that a tornado had in fact occurred. Only minor damage and no deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure area in the panhandle of Oklahoma and an advancing warm front and dry line into Southern Kansas, lead to the development of supercell thunderstorms across portions of Southeast Kansas.  The supercells moved rapidly north out of Northern Oklahoma into Southern Kansas producing numerous reports of quarter size hail and some isolated wind damage.

The convection pushed eastward into Missouri by early afternoon, but strong pressure gradient winds with sustained speeds of 25-35 mph and gusts to 55-65 mph persisted through much of the afternoon across northeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No reports of damage were received in the county, but up to 60 mph winds were estimated by county law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system brought strong gradient winds through central and eastern Kansas on Monday, March 23rd.  Strong gradient winds were enhanced by a low level jet core of 65 knots 2,000 ft. off of the surface.  These strong synoptic conditions combined to bring widespread wind damage across the Topeka County Warning Area.  Wind gusts frequently climbed to 60 mph, and highest gusts topped out above 70 mph, without the presence of convection.  Newspaper reports noted several semi-truck drivers driving along Interstate 70 between Ellsworth and Riley Counties were injured when their vehicles overturned.  Only one injury of such was confirmed to have occurred in the Topeka county warning area, five miles west of Topeka. Several newspaper reports noted debris flying around in cities and towns across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down between 4th and 5th streets in Osage City.  The tree knocked down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm of record breaking proportions struck central, south-central and southeast Kansas March 27-28, 2009. Heavy snow with blizzard conditions affected much of central and south-central Kansas, with accumulations exceeding 18 inches for some locations. Numerous buildings with mainly flat-topped roofs received various degrees of roof damage, some of which was rather extensive due to the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Many travelers became stranded due to the deep snow and blizzard conditions, some of which needed rescue by the National Guard. Meanwhile, sleet and freezing rain was the main culprit farther east across portions of south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas. Sleet accumulations up to 4 inches and ice accumulations up to three-quarters of an inch downed numerous trees, tree limbs, power poles and power lines, causing tens of thousands of power outages. The snow packed and icy roads aided in an uncountable number of auto accidents areawide. Two fatalities were attributed to the winter storm, with several injuries, most of which were due to auto accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow, with periodic bouts of heavy snow, affected the county from the early morning hours on the 27th through the early afternoon hours on the 28th. However, eastern and southern portions of the county received a wintry mix of snow and sleet through much of the day on the 27th, changing over to all snow from west to east during the evening and overnight hours. Snowfall amounts over western and northern portions of the county ranged from roughly 12 to 20 inches, while southern and eastern portions of the county received roughly 6 to 12 inches of snow on top of 2 to 3 inches of sleet. Cunningham received 15 to 20 inches of snow, while Kingman received 9 to 12 inches. Stout north to northeast winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of snow, with visibilities occasionally dropping to one-quarter mile or less. Portions of the roof on a Kingman grocery store collapsed underneath the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Portions of the roof on the Kingman Activity Center collapsed as well. The Cunningham Courier and the Kingman Leader-Courier contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard impacted much of southwest Kansas with record or near record snowfall recorded in some areas. There was a strip from southeast Clark county to Pratt county that had 28 to 31 inches of snow and 10 to 15 foot drifts.  There was also a heavy band of snow in Grant county to Meade county that had over 20 inches of snow.  Lesser amounts occurred elsewhere but winds of 40 to 60 mph produced 10 to 20 foot drifts across the majority of the area.  Most of the snow fell in a 24 hour period.  Many highways were shut down for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 21.1 inches fell 16 miles southeast of Willmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather event took shape over northeast Kansas, with the expected type of accumulating hydrometeor at the forefront of the challenge. Originally, the airmass over northeast Kansas looked capable of supporting snow through the duration of the winter weather event.  But, a belt of strong moist southerly flow continuously advected into the region, and a warm moist midlevel layer supported more of a sleet or freezing rain type scenario over mainly east central Kansas.  Snow was the predominant precipitation type further west, and was eventually seen in every county of the NWS-Topeka warning area, but sleet and ice accumulations played a larger role than originally thought. Several road crews were noted to say though that because of the heavy, wet nature of the snow, it had been easy to remove. Power outages were noted across portions of the region, notably in Dickinson and Geary Counties.  Several road slide-offs were reported across the area, but no injuries or fatalities. Many of the accidents occurred on bridges and overpasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Primarily rain fell during the evening hours of the 27th, but gradually transitioned to a mix of freezing rain and sleet, then eventually snow through the night. Half an inch of ice was reported to have accumulated in the southern part of the county, which one quarter of an inch was reported across much of the rest of the county.  Power lines were reported down in and around Gridley, tree limbs snapped off in Burlingame as a result of the weight of the accumulated ice, and several slide-offs were reported by law enforcement along I-35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread very strong southerly winds affected much of central and south-central Kansas from the late morning through the afternoon hours on the 23rd, in response to a strong dry line and cold front approaching from the west and northwest. Widespread sustained wind speeds were between 40 and 45 mph, with numerous gusts around 60 mph. Structural damage was relatively minor, limited mainly to shingle and trim damage. However, a portion of the roof from the Wiley building in downtown Hutchinson peeled off, blocking off main street for a time. Many semi-trucks overturned on east-west running highways, including Highways 50 and 56, and Interstate 70. Several power poles and power lines were downed across the area, resulting in thousands of power outages. The downed power lines also ignited rapidly spreading grass fires. However, little is known regarding the coverage, magnitude or severity of these fires. As the afternoon progressed, strong to severe thunderstorms ignited along the advancing dry line and cold front, producing up to half dollar size hail, 70 mph winds and a handful of brief, relatively weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds inflicted roof damage to the Wiley building in downtown Hutchinson, blocking off main street for a time. Roof and shingle damage was reported 5 miles east of Sylvia, and two semi-trucks were blown over on Highway 50 between Yoder Road and K-96. At least six power poles were downed throughout the county, which caused several grass fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm of record breaking proportions struck central, south-central and southeast Kansas March 27-28, 2009. Heavy snow with blizzard conditions affected much of central and south-central Kansas, with accumulations exceeding 18 inches for some locations. Numerous buildings with mainly flat-topped roofs received various degrees of roof damage, some of which was rather extensive due to the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Many travelers became stranded due to the deep snow and blizzard conditions, some of which needed rescue by the National Guard. Meanwhile, sleet and freezing rain was the main culprit farther east across portions of south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas. Sleet accumulations up to 4 inches and ice accumulations up to three-quarters of an inch downed numerous trees, tree limbs, power poles and power lines, causing tens of thousands of power outages. The snow packed and icy roads aided in an uncountable number of auto accidents areawide. Two fatalities were attributed to the winter storm, with several injuries, most of which were due to auto accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of light to moderate sleet, snow and periodic freezing rain affected the county during the day on the 27th. The wintry mix eventually changed over to all snow from west to east during the evening and overnight hours, and persisted into the afternoon hours on the 28th. Meaningful ice and sleet accumulations were confined to mainly the eastern three-fourths of the county, where sleet accumulated over 2 to 3 inches in some places. Meanwhile, snowfall accumulations exceeded 12 inches over extreme west and northwest portions of the county, where stout north to northeast winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of snow, with visibilities occasionally dropping to one-quarter mile or less. Total ice, sleet and snow accumulations over eastern portions of the county ranged from roughly 4 to 8 inches. Treacherous travel conditions lead to multiple accidents in and around Newton, some of which involved injuries, but none were critical. Portions of the roof of a Hesston plant collapsed underneath the weight of the heavy, wet snow. The combination of ice, snow and wind downed several power lines countywide. The Newton Kansan contributed to this report.

The wintry mix eventually changed over to snow during the evening and overnight hours from west to east, and persisted into the afternoon hours on the 28th. Sleet accumulations across the county ranged from roughly 2 to 4 inches, with 6 to 15 inches of snow, highest over northwest portions of the county, accumulating on top of the sleet from the evening and overnight hours on the 27th and beyond. Stout north to northeast winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of snow, with visibilities occasionally dropping to one-quarter mile or less. The combination of ice, snow and wind downed around 18 power poles across the county. Additionally, a couple businesses, one in Harper and the other just north of Attica received damage when portions of their roofs collapsed underneath the weight of the heavy, wet snow. There was one reported car port and front porch awning collapse across the county. The Harper Advocate and Hutchinson News contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather event took shape over northeast Kansas, with the expected type of accumulating hydrometeor at the forefront of the challenge. Originally, the airmass over northeast Kansas looked capable of supporting snow through the duration of the winter weather event.  But, a belt of strong moist southerly flow continuously advected into the region, and a warm moist midlevel layer supported more of a sleet or freezing rain type scenario over mainly east central Kansas.  Snow was the predominant precipitation type further west, and was eventually seen in every county of the NWS-Topeka warning area, but sleet and ice accumulations played a larger role than originally thought. Several road crews were noted to say though that because of the heavy, wet nature of the snow, it had been easy to remove. Power outages were noted across portions of the region, notably in Dickinson and Geary Counties.  Several road slide-offs were reported across the area, but no injuries or fatalities. Many of the accidents occurred on bridges and overpasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice and snow reports were slow to trickle into the NWS-TOP, but came fast and furiously after 9am CST as a heavy band of precipitation began to affect the county. Snow, freezing rain, and sleet were all reported, and sites across the county reported ice accumulations beginning around 9am CST, with half of an inch of ice reported on trees and power lines at some locations before 10am CST.  An inch of sleet accumulated four miles southeast of Council Grove, while storm total accumulations across the rest of the county were around half an inch of sleet, and one to two inches of snow.

Eight-tenths of an inch of ice was measured to have accumulated north-northwest of Osage City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread very strong southerly winds affected much of central and south-central Kansas from the late morning through the afternoon hours on the 23rd, in response to a strong dry line and cold front approaching from the west and northwest. Widespread sustained wind speeds were between 40 and 45 mph, with numerous gusts around 60 mph. Structural damage was relatively minor, limited mainly to shingle and trim damage. However, a portion of the roof from the Wiley building in downtown Hutchinson peeled off, blocking off main street for a time. Many semi-trucks overturned on east-west running highways, including Highways 50 and 56, and Interstate 70. Several power poles and power lines were downed across the area, resulting in thousands of power outages. The downed power lines also ignited rapidly spreading grass fires. However, little is known regarding the coverage, magnitude or severity of these fires. As the afternoon progressed, strong to severe thunderstorms ignited along the advancing dry line and cold front, producing up to half dollar size hail, 70 mph winds and a handful of brief, relatively weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by AWOS equipment at the McPherson Airport. The strong winds downed a few power poles and power lines across the county. A semi-truck overturned near the intersection of Highway 153 and 61. The McPherson Sentinel contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow, with periodic bouts of heavy snow, affected the county from the early morning hours on the 27th through the early afternoon hours on the 28th. However, southeast portions of the county received a wintry mix of snow and sleet through much of the day on the 27th, changing over to all snow during the evening and overnight hours. Widespread snowfall amounts ranged from roughly 12 to 20 inches across the county. Hutchinson received 16 inches, with western portions of the county reaching 20 inches. Southeast portions of the county received 7 to 12 inches of snow, on top of at least 1 to 2 inches of sleet. Stout north to northeast winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of snow, with visibilities occasionally dropping to one-quarter mile or less. Three known injuries occurred in two automobile accidents due to the adverse road conditions. Unfortunately, an Arlington man died of a heart attack caused by shoveling snow. Paramedics were unable to reach him in a timely manner due to nearly impassable roads. Additionally, the heavy, wet snow caused a string of roof collapses and/or roof damage, including several businesses and a few schools. A canopy at the State Fairgrounds collapsed as well, along with some residential outbuildings and sheds. The Hutchinson News and Salina Journal contributed to this report.

Several newspaper reports noted debris flying around in cities and towns across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sign was blown off of the roof of a building and traveled through the window of an adjacent building.  No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread very strong southerly winds affected much of central and south-central Kansas from the late morning through the afternoon hours on the 23rd, in response to a strong dry line and cold front approaching from the west and northwest. Widespread sustained wind speeds were between 40 and 45 mph, with numerous gusts around 60 mph. Structural damage was relatively minor, limited mainly to shingle and trim damage. However, a portion of the roof from the Wiley building in downtown Hutchinson peeled off, blocking off main street for a time. Many semi-trucks overturned on east-west running highways, including Highways 50 and 56, and Interstate 70. Several power poles and power lines were downed across the area, resulting in thousands of power outages. The downed power lines also ignited rapidly spreading grass fires. However, little is known regarding the coverage, magnitude or severity of these fires. As the afternoon progressed, strong to severe thunderstorms ignited along the advancing dry line and cold front, producing up to half dollar size hail, 70 mph winds and a handful of brief, relatively weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one semi-truck was blown over on I-70. The roof of a shed at a lumber supply company in Ellsworth was blown off. The Ellsworth County Independent Reporter contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another windy day across southwest Kansas as strong low pressure moved into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56 mph wind gust was recorded by the KSN Weather Lab at the Ingalls high school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced high wind in Liberal.

A deep low pressure system brought strong gradient winds through central and eastern Kansas on Monday, March 23rd.  Strong gradient winds were enhanced by a low level jet core of 65 knots 2,000 ft. off of the surface.  These strong synoptic conditions combined to bring widespread wind damage across the Topeka County Warning Area.  Wind gusts frequently climbed to 60 mph, and highest gusts topped out above 70 mph, without the presence of convection.  Newspaper reports noted several semi-truck drivers driving along Interstate 70 between Ellsworth and Riley Counties were injured when their vehicles overturned.  Only one injury of such was confirmed to have occurred in the Topeka county warning area, five miles west of Topeka. Several newspaper reports noted debris flying around in cities and towns across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a barn roof was blown off at US 59 and 150th st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather event took shape over northeast Kansas, with the expected type of accumulating hydrometeor at the forefront of the challenge. Originally, the airmass over northeast Kansas looked capable of supporting snow through the duration of the winter weather event.  But, a belt of strong moist southerly flow continuously advected into the region, and a warm moist midlevel layer supported more of a sleet or freezing rain type scenario over mainly east central Kansas.  Snow was the predominant precipitation type further west, and was eventually seen in every county of the NWS-Topeka warning area, but sleet and ice accumulations played a larger role than originally thought. Several road crews were noted to say though that because of the heavy, wet nature of the snow, it had been easy to remove. Power outages were noted across portions of the region, notably in Dickinson and Geary Counties.  Several road slide-offs were reported across the area, but no injuries or fatalities. Many of the accidents occurred on bridges and overpasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy precipitation lifted through Riley County overnight the 27th into the 28th of March. A mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow--heavy at times--was reported with this band.  Total sleet accumulations reached up to 2 inches, while snow totals up to seven inches were reported in the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The interaction of a quasistationary front and a dryline lead to discrete supercells developing across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of March 7th, 2009. Brief tornado touchdowns occured just to the south of Hutchinson with the supercells along with hail up to golfball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was reported just to the west of the intersection of highways 61 and 50. The tornado touched down in an open field with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low topped convection developed during the early morning hours of February 9th in the warm sector ahead of an advancing cold front as an upper trough moved through the region. Several reports of wind damage were reported across mainly southeastern portions of the county warning area.

The convection pushed eastward into Missouri by early afternoon, but strong pressure gradient winds with sustained speeds of 25-35 mph and gusts to 55-65 mph persisted through much of the afternoon across northeast Kansas.
